caveats: a: The closest occupation shares cover five-digit NAICS 31194 rather than 311942 alone.
a: The source does not identify already-automated tasks, so that exclusion is judgmental.
a: GenAI exposure research may miss machine vision, predictive maintenance, and process-control AI.
rho: BTOS covers all industries and business functions, including low-intensity uses that do not yield labor substitution.
rho: AI-related headcount effects to date need not predict avoidable hiring over five years.
rho: Spice-specific safety obligations do not bar automation but raise the cost of validation and oversight.
e: No public source directly measures eligible firms in 311942.
e: McCormick is global, public, and far larger than the target band.
e: Customized products qualify only when supplied as a repeat outsourced capability, not merely when sold as catalog ingredients.
s5: Owner-age evidence is old and not industry-specific.
s5: BizBuySell reports a broad other-manufacturing category and only transactions disclosed to its marketplace.
s5: The range excludes internal reorganizations and non-transferable closures.
q: A public global supplier's contracts and bargaining power may differ from private LMM firms.
q: Retention should be higher for proprietary custom flavor systems and lower for standard spices or private-label bids.
q: The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation failure, which belong in other primitives.
d5: McCormick's geography, product scope, brand mix, and size differ from the lens.
d5: Real foodservice spending is not a physical-volume measure for spice ingredients.
d5: Reformulation can reduce ingredient weight while preserving flavor-service value, complicating quantity measurement.
o: No source measures the share of year-five quantity requiring this operator type.
o: Accountability may move between competing manufacturers without eliminating the operator role.
o: Highly standardized spices may be sourced directly, while custom blends and extracts have stronger operator dependence.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring business-to-business ingredient and finished-product sales, commonly based on customer formulas, specifications, private labels, or application requirements, with pricing and quantities set through purchase orders, master agreements, or frequently renewed annual arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Consumer-brand-only businesses without recurring outsourced production, commodity trading without manufacturing, coffee or meat extracts, industrial salts, captive plants, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. firms repeatedly manufacturing custom or private-label spices, table salt, seasonings, non-coffee and non-meat flavoring extracts, natural food colorings, and qualifying dry food mixes for external food manufacturers, foodservice operators, distributors, or retailers.

a.bridge: A published NAICS 31194 occupation summary, a broader group containing both 311941 and 311942, attributes 44% of employment to production, 18% to transportation/material moving, 9% to office support, 6% to maintenance, and 5% to management. ILO task research finds GenAI exposure concentrated in clerical and digitized work. I apply relatively high task exposure to office, commercial, formulation-support, procurement, planning, and documentation roles; modest exposure to management and technical quality work; and low exposure to physical production, sanitation, and material handling, with an upward wage-weighting for professional roles. Including wet-sauce plants in the occupational proxy may overstate packaging and sanitation labor versus dry blending and extract operations, potentially biasing exposure downward.
caveats: The closest occupation shares cover five-digit NAICS 31194 rather than 311942 alone.
The source does not identify already-automated tasks, so that exclusion is judgmental.
GenAI exposure research may miss machine vision, predictive maintenance, and process-control AI.
a.change_evidence: A 311942 payroll-by-SOC extract with wages, establishment interviews mapping time by task, and a plant-level inventory distinguishing existing controls and robotics from still-manual planning, laboratory, QA, commercial, and line tasks.
a.rationale: Spice and extract plants combine physical receiving, treatment, milling, blending, filling, sanitation, and warehousing with comparatively valuable formulation, specification, sourcing, quality, sales, and regulatory work. AI can substitute parts of the latter workflows and improve vision, forecasting, and exception handling, but it cannot directly perform most material transformation and hygienic plant work.

d5.bridge: McCormick is broader and global, but its 2025 filing reports total volume/mix growth of 1.2%, Consumer volume/mix growth of 2.1%, and Flavor Solutions volume/mix decline of 0.2%, alongside decades-long customized customer relationships and demand across eating occasions. USDA reports long-run real growth in U.S. foodservice spending, while Census reports U.S. population growth of 0.5% from mid-2024 to mid-2025. I translate these adjacent signals into modest five-year U.S. constant-quality quantity growth. Revenue mix and a single public company do not isolate 311942 physical output, and foodservice spending may overstate ingredient volume.
caveats: McCormick's geography, product scope, brand mix, and size differ from the lens.
Real foodservice spending is not a physical-volume measure for spice ingredients.
Reformulation can reduce ingredient weight while preserving flavor-service value, complicating quantity measurement.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Circana or NielsenIQ unit trends for retail spices and mixes, Technomic case-volume forecasts, shipment indices for flavor ingredients, and target-customer forecasts normalized for formulation concentration and quality changes.
d5.rationale: Spices, seasoning blends, extracts, colors, and dry mixes are low-dose inputs used across retail food, packaged food, and foodservice. Population, new flavor launches, and outsourcing support modest growth, while mature household usage, customer insourcing, reformulation, and restaurant or packaged-food weakness constrain it.

e.bridge: McCormick's broad global flavor business reports that its Flavor Solutions segment, which supplies customized products to food manufacturers and foodservice customers, represented 42% of 2025 net sales; it also identifies private-label supply in its Consumer segment. This is revenue mix for one large diversified industry participant, not an LMM firm-count share. I use it to show that recurring external flavor solutions are a substantial industry model, then adjust for smaller custom blenders likely to be service-oriented and for own-brand, commodity-salt, and retail-spice firms that are not eligible. McCormick's scale and branded portfolio could bias the direct customized share either downward relative to specialists or upward relative to small commodity producers.
caveats: No public source directly measures eligible firms in 311942.
McCormick is global, public, and far larger than the target band.
Customized products qualify only when supplied as a repeat outsourced capability, not merely when sold as catalog ingredients.
e.change_evidence: Revenue-by-customer-program data for the estimated 123 LMM-band firms, classifying custom formula, customer-owned formula, private label, standard ingredient, and own-brand revenue, with tests of recurrence, customer tenure, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: Customized seasoning blends, flavors, extracts, dry mixes, and private-label packs often embody an outsourced development and production relationship. However, the NAICS code also contains own-brand spice businesses and standardized commodity products, so not every LMM firm qualifies.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311942 ($2,460,355,139) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311942 ($13,940,546,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $602,756 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311942); class <5: 145 firms, avg revenue/firm $749,938 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $114,366 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,780,358 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $271,505 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,442,479 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $829,978 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 74 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,934,527 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,887,515 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $60,932,510 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,292,208 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $221,691,683 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,807,982 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 123

caveats: No source measures the share of year-five quantity requiring this operator type.
Accountability may move between competing manufacturers without eliminating the operator role.
Highly standardized spices may be sourced directly, while custom blends and extracts have stronger operator dependence.
o.change_evidence: Procurement interviews with food manufacturers and foodservice chains on planned insourcing and direct sourcing, plus evidence on autonomous blending plants and whether equipment or software providers will assume food-release, supplier-verification, and recall liability.
o.rationale: Software can recommend formulas, automate documents, and help control production, but customers still need an accountable entity to source globally, qualify suppliers, treat pathogens, mill, blend, extract, test, pack, release, trace, and recall physical food ingredients. FDA's spice findings and preventive-control requirements make accountable supply-chain and manufacturing oversight persistent. Limited customer insourcing or simplified direct sourcing could remove some outsourced volume.

q.bridge: McCormick reports that key price and quantity terms are established frequently and most customer arrangements last one year or less; it also describes customer price adjustments, price elasticity, and concentrated Flavor Solutions accounts. These are observed mechanisms at a large comparable spice-and-flavor supplier, not a direct measure of retained AI benefit. I model meaningful retention within a term or in differentiated custom formulations, then reduce it for annual repricing, customer procurement leverage, competitive rebids, and shared productivity. McCormick's unusually large customers may overstate pass-through pressure for niche LMM accounts, biasing the retained-share estimate downward.
caveats: A public global supplier's contracts and bargaining power may differ from private LMM firms.
Retention should be higher for proprietary custom flavor systems and lower for standard spices or private-label bids.
The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation failure, which belong in other primitives.
q.change_evidence: Target contract and invoice cohorts showing term, termination, cost-index, productivity-sharing, open-book, exclusivity, formula ownership, and repricing provisions, linked to gross-margin retention through multiple renewals after automation projects.
q.rationale: Custom formulas, sensory expertise, food-safety qualification, and switching work let the operator retain part of productivity gains. Yet frequent price setting, powerful food customers, ingredient-cost adjustments, and competitive alternative suppliers allow a substantial portion to pass through over five years.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS research reports 18% of firms using AI in a business function in late 2025/early 2026, 32% on an employment-weighted basis, while 66% of users relied on AI only to augment tasks and only 2% reported AI-related employment decreases. I convert these economy-wide adoption and depth observations into a five-year implementation share, allowing faster rollout in document-heavy formulation and quality workflows but discounting smaller-plant data fragmentation, validation, cyber risk, and change costs. National and large-firm patterns likely overstate implementation capacity in LMM spice manufacturers.
caveats: BTOS covers all industries and business functions, including low-intensity uses that do not yield labor substitution.
AI-related headcount effects to date need not predict avoidable hiring over five years.
Spice-specific safety obligations do not bar automation but raise the cost of validation and oversight.
rho.change_evidence: A 311942 operator survey of AI deployments by workflow, implementation spend, validated error rates, customer approvals, labor hours removed or avoided, and production systems that vendors can integrate within five years.
rho.rationale: Proven uses in specification comparison, certificates of analysis, supplier-risk review, formulation search, forecasting, scheduling, customer response, and maintenance support can spread within five years. Pathogen controls, sensory decisions, laboratory verification, customer qualification, and older production equipment keep much of the exposed opportunity from becoming operational savings quickly.

s5.bridge: Census linked single-unit employer-firm research reports 49.55% of primary owners older than 54 in its 2007 sample, and BizBuySell reports 83 closed other-manufacturing transactions in 2025. I translate broad aging and observed small-manufacturing liquidity into a five-year control-transfer probability, discounting for stale age data, marketplace undercoverage, transactions outside the target EBITDA band, and spice businesses that fail food-safety, customer-concentration, or transferability diligence. The old age vintage may understate succession pressure, while marketplace counts materially understate private transfers.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is old and not industry-specific.
BizBuySell reports a broad other-manufacturing category and only transactions disclosed to its marketplace.
The range excludes internal reorganizations and non-transferable closures.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched dataset of PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a), broker, and industry-association transactions for spice, seasoning, extract, and dry-mix manufacturers, combined with owner age and failed-sale reasons for the target cohort.
s5.rationale: A large estimated LMM cohort and succession pressure create plausible acquisition flow, but completed transfers remain episodic. Specialized sourcing relationships, proprietary formulas, key-person sensory knowledge, customer qualification, pathogen-control records, and facility investment can make otherwise willing sellers non-transferable.

principal_driver: Recurring custom formulations and safety-qualified customer relationships preserve operator demand while creating document-, data-, and knowledge-intensive workflows suited to AI.
principal_weakness: Eligibility and labor exposure are inferred from a large public flavor company and broader-industry occupation data rather than measured for private LMM spice and extract firms.
